mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E320 CDI or E350 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM642 3.0-litre V6 diesel engine typically costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £1,500–£3,500. A full engine replacement can cost anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E320 CDI or E350 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 is considered a solid diesel overall, but it has well-documented issues. The most notorious is oil cooler seal failure — a labour-intensive repair that can cost over £2,000. Other common issues include swirl flap motor failure, exhaust manifold cracking, turbocharger problems, and glow plug breakage. The OM642 was also part of the Dieselgate scandal. With proper maintenance, these engines can be very durable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E320 CDI and E350 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are oil cooler seal failure (oil leaks from the V of the engine, high labour costs), swirl flap motor failure (engine warning light P2015, power loss), exhaust manifold cracking (loss of power, turbo damage), turbocharger actuator failure, glow plug breakage (M8 threads snap on removal), and main bearing failure on pre-2009 engines. Regular maintenance using MB 229.51 spec oil is essential.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E-Class CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £8,000 E350 CDI, a £4,500 recon is 56% — approaching our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a tired £3,500 example, the same recon exceeds the threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained E-Class CDIs are becoming rarer — a proper restoration is often justified for a clean example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E320 CDI or E350 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W211 (2005–2009): OM642.920 / .921. W212 (2009–2016): OM642.850 / .852 / .858. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 'E 320 CDI' or 'E 350 CDI'.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the oil cooler seals on the OM642, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — oil cooler seal replacement is a well-understood but labour-intensive repair costing £800–£2,000. The oil cooler is located within the V of the engine, beneath a host of ancillary parts which need to be removed before the cooler can be accessed. The purple Viton seals last about 10–15 years or 100,000 miles. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the oil cooler seals inspected immediately. Ignoring this can lead to low oil levels and potential engine damage.

eraMap.title: The E320 / E350 CDI THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: The original OM642 in the E-Class — 224 hp, 540 Nm. Smooth and torquey, but oil cooler seals and swirl flaps are known weak points.
The refined OM642 — up to 265 hp, 620 Nm. More power, better efficiency, but oil cooler seals and exhaust manifold cracks remain concerns.

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes E320 / E350 CDI Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available from Mercedes — the OM642 is out of production

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the OM642 3.0-litre V6 diesel engine. A full engine replacement can cost anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Reconditioned OM642 engines are available from approximately £3,000–£5,500. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E-Class OM642 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The V6's packaging in the engine bay makes this a moderately complex job.

cta.label: → Compare E-Class diesel engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Oil Cooler Seal Failure — The OM642's Achilles Heel
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from the engine bay, low oil level warning, oil traces in the inner V of the engine (between cylinder banks), visible oil residue around the oil cooler area.
problems.replacementCost: £3,000–£7,500 (if left untreated leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: Oil cooler seal failure is the most notorious issue on the OM642. The oil cooler is located within the V of the engine, beneath a host of ancillary parts which need to be removed before the cooler can be accessed – hence the high labour cost. When the seals degrade, they cause lubricant to leak from the oil cooler. The purple Viton seals fitted to later engines last about 10–15 years or 100,000 miles. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the oil cooler seals inspected immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

problems.title: Swirl Flap Motor Failure
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light (P2015), poor idle, power loss, delayed acceleration, failed emissions test.
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 (swirl flap motor replacement or bypass resistor)
problems.replacementCost: £3,000–£7,500 (if left untreated leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The OM642's swirl flap system uses an external operating rod made of poor-quality plastic which wears prematurely. Oil can reach the servo motor through the air hose, causing failure. A common fix is a bypass resistor that fools the computer — the cheapest and easiest fix. However, if the swirl flaps themselves are damaged, intake manifold replacement may be required. If you experience power loss or an engine warning light, have the swirl flap system inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full swirl flap failure guide

problems.title: Exhaust Manifold Cracking & Turbocharger Issues
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, unusual noises from the engine bay, rattling on cold start, turbo lag, exhaust smells in the cabin.
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,500 (exhaust manifold replacement or turbo actuator repair)
problems.replacementCost: £3,000–£7,500 (if turbo failure leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The exhaust manifold is one of the most common weak points of the OM642. The extreme temperatures in the exhaust area can cause cracks or leaks. This can not only reduce performance but also damage the turbocharger in the long term. Workshop costs for replacement can quickly run into the thousands. The turbocharger actuator can also fail, causing bucking and loss of power. If you notice loss of power or unusual noises, have the exhaust manifold and turbocharger inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E320 / E350 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Well-understood but labour-intensive — catch it early
Bypass resistor is cheap and effective
Catch it before it damages the turbo
Cleaning or replacement — cheaper than full turbo replacement
M8×1 threads can snap during removal

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E-Class CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with evidence of regular oil changes using MB 229.51 spec oil
* Oil cooler seals replaced within the last 40,000 miles
* Swirl flap motor replaced or bypassed
* Post-2009 W212 preferred over early W211 (improved main bearings)
* No cold-start rattle, loss of power, or oil leaks
avoidIf: * Oil spots under the car or oil smell in the cabin (oil cooler seals)
* Engine warning light with swirl flap or turbo codes
* Loss of power or unusual exhaust noises (exhaust manifold cracks)
* Poor service history — the OM642 is a sophisticated engine that demands good maintenance
* Pre-2009 W211 with no history of main bearing inspection

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E320 CDI and E350 CDI are powered by Mercedes' OM642 V6 diesel — a smooth, torquey, and refined engine that served across the E-Class for over a decade. It's a solid diesel overall, but it has known weak points: oil cooler seal failure is the most notorious; swirl flap motors, exhaust manifolds, and turbochargers also demand attention. These are well-understood and manageable with proper maintenance. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained E-Class CDI is a £5,000–£15,000 executive saloon; a neglected one is a £5,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare E-Class diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes E320 CDI or E350 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or unusual noises (timing chain or turbo warning)
* Service History — including the OM642 era (2005–2016) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.51 spec oil
* Oil Leak Check — Check for oil spots under the car or oil traces in the V of the engine (oil cooler seals)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any swirl flap (P2015), turbo, or EGR codes?
* Power Check — Test acceleration; any loss of power or lag? (turbo or exhaust manifold warning)
* Smoke Check — Black smoke can indicate turbo or injector issues; white smoke can indicate coolant issues
* Glow Plug Check — Any history of glow plug replacement? (M8 threads can snap on removal)
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
